sourceRef.citation: Kourovka Notebook, Problem 19.50 (Khukhro & Mazurov eds., ed.19)

statement.latex: A finite graph is said to be integral if all eigenvalues of its adjacency matrix are integers. [r]{a)} Let$G$be a finite group generated by a normal subset$R$consisting of involutions. Is it true that the Cayley graph$Cay(G,R)$is integral? [r]{b)} Let$A_n$be the alternating group of degree$n$, let$S=\{(123),\, (124), ,(12n)\}$and$R=S S^{-1}$. Is it true that the Cayley graph$Cay(A_n,R)$is integral...

reiAssessment.solveRationale: Group theory I_INFINITE_SEARCH; Rei not specialized in group theory (yet)
reiAssessment.formalizationNotes: Kourovka problems are typically research-level group theory
